An airstrike is reported in Loi Hkwin (လွယ်ခွင်) village, Nawnghkio (နောင်ချို) township on 1 April 2025 around 11:20 local time, injuring five people. The village, reportedly controlled by the TNLA, saw houses destroyed, including a fertiliser factory.

Myanmar Witness assessment: A likely location of the incident is found.
attributes.Town_Village: Loi Hkwin (လွယ်ခွင်)

attributes.Description:
An airstrike is reported in Mindat (မင်းတပ်) town, Mindat township twice, at around 18:00 and 22:30 local time on 1 April 2025, despite no ongoing combat, while the National Mourning ceremony for those affected by the earthquake was taking place. The bombings allegedly damaged buildings and residential areas; but no casualties were reported.

Myanmar Witness assessment: No verification has been possible. Location on map is based on claims.

An airstrike is reported in the southern area of Kawkareik (ကော့ကရိတ်) township on 1 April 2025 at 16:52 local time.

Myanmar Witness assessment: No verification has been possible. Location on map is based on claims.

attributes.Public_Description:
Shwe Kokko (水沟谷), also known as Yatai New City, is located on the left bank of the Moei River, north of Myawaddy, Kayin State, at approximately [16.820472, 98.530917]. The location has been widely acknowledged online as a scam compound, and satellite imagery confirms extensive development activity beginning around 2018. 

Shwe Kokko is a Chinese-associated development project. Reporting cited in the provided material states that the Yatai New City Project resulted from an agreement signed in July 2017 between the Karen Border Guard Force (Karen BGF) and Hong Kong–registered Yatai International Holdings Group. The project has also been referred to as a special economic zone (SEZ). Multiple sources link the project to Chinese business interests and criminal networks, including She Zhijiang, chairman of Yatai International Holding Group. She Zhijiang has been described as developing a 15 billion dollar complex in Shwe Kokko and was arrested in Thailand in August 2022. The Shwe Kokko project is reportedly under the control of the Karen BGF at the time of writing. 

Regarding the compound, Business and Human Rights Resource Centre raised concerns about reports of drug trafficking, illicit finance, and partnerships between Chinese firms and local armed groups.

Myanmar Witness’s analysis of satellite imagery reveals multiple indicators consistent with a high-security compound. These include surrounding walls and fencing, internal sectioning, repetitive prison-like residential structures, watchtower-like features along internal and external perimeters, large multi-storey buildings capable of housing many people, and gated access points. An enclosed central area appears potentially suitable for farming, a feature comparable to labour camps and prison facilities elsewhere in Myanmar. Additional structures identified as possible staff housing are located nearby at approximately [16.813777, 98.539624]. Myanmar Witness's investigation identifies potential informal border-crossing points along the Moei River near Shwe Kokko. These crossings are highlighted as strategically significant and consistent with patterns seen at other scam and trafficking-linked compounds along the border.

Based on satellite imagery analysis, geolocation of user-generated content (UGC) and comparisons with known detention and labour facilities in Myanmar, Myanmar Witness assesses Shwe Kokko as a high-security compound. The site is not formally recognised as a labour camp by the government, but the infrastructure and proximity to potential informal border crossings strongly suggests the capacity to detain and control people against their will. Given the site’s widely reported association with scam operations, armed group involvement, and illicit economies, Myanmar Witness concludes that Shwe Kokko certainly functions, or has functioned, as a scam compound and can be described as a form of modern, technology-facilitated labour camp.

[Figure: Myanmar Witness analysis using Google Earth, ©Multiple Years CNES/Airbus]

attributes.NAME__MY___comma_separated_: အပိုလိုကမ်းပက်စ်
attributes.Public_Description:
Apollo Campus (阿波羅園區), located in Myawaddy, Kayin State, was first seen on satellite imagery in 2019. Multiple sources have mentioned the site as a scam compound, including Justice for Myanmar. It is considered part of a broader network of criminal cyber-fraud compounds along the Thai–Myanmar border. The compound is operated under Myanmar Apollo International Investment Company Limited. 

Shareholders of Apollo Park reportedly include individuals linked to the armed group Karen BGF, through proxies such as Saw Htoo Eh Moo and Saw Soe Hlaing Oo. The site once maintained a Chinese-language Facebook page listing job recruitment advertisements, some of which bear the hallmarks of scam recruitment. The page included photos showing a series of low-rise office blocks, a nightclub, and a convenience store. Myanmar Witness assesses the likelihood of Apollo Campus being a scam compound as almost inevitable, based on satellite imagery, media references, and social media sources.

Internal fortification is evident with one large wall and gates separating the compound from roads and other buildings in the area. On the ground, indicators suggest that Apollo Campus functions as a fortified compound capable of controlling movement, potentially segregating residents into living and working quarters while restricting their freedom to move outside. OpenStreetMap directly labels armed guards within the compound. The site exhibits similarities with known scam parks, further linking it to broader scam, fraud, and trafficking operations. Apollo Campus is located in the Shwe Koko area, adjacent to the Thai-Myanmar border. The site’s location provides a strategic advantage for cross-border operations along the Shwe Koko–Mae Sot corridor.

Publicly verified claims tie Apollo Park to human trafficking and forced labour operations. The 2025 case of Chinese actor Wang Xing illustrates the operational methods: he was lured to Thailand under the pretence of an acting job, abducted, and transported to Myawaddy. Wang Xing was held initially with around 10 others, then transferred to a larger “training centre” within the park, holding at least 50 Chinese detainees. He was forced to participate in scam operations, and his kidnapping and coercion forced an overseas crackdown by regional authorities. Following the high-profile Wang Xing case, Apollo Park may now be inactive. Still, Myanmar Witness continues to monitor the site due to its historical links to human trafficking, forced labour, and cyber-fraud operations, as well as claims as recently as February 2026 that people are being trafficked to the compound under false job pretences for labour and exploitation. Given all these factors, Myanmar Witness concludes that this compound is certainly one that functions, or has functioned, as a scam compound.

[Figure: OpenStreetMap]

attributes.NAME__MY___comma_separated_: ကျင်းရှင်း ပက်ခ်
attributes.Public_Description:
Jinxin Park (金鑫园区), located in Myawaddy, Kayin State, at approximately [16.713794, 98.479683] was first reported to have been constructed in 2019. Myanmar Witness determines the likelihood of this site being a scam compound as almost certain. The park has been associated with cyber-scam activity and is referenced in international reporting on scam compounds in Southeast Asia, including a May 2025 report by the Global Initiative Against Transnational Organised Crime. 

Jinxin Park is alleged to have been operated by an individual known as Xiong Shijia and to have been established and managed by Jinxin Holding Company Limited, a Myanmar-registered entity. The company had two listed directors: Cao Guolei, a Chinese national from Anhui Province, and Shwe Htay, a Myanmar national with a registered address in Lashio. Although Jinxin Holding Company Limited was dissolved in 2022 under Myanmar’s Insolvency Law, the park is reported to have continued operating thereafter. An additional Chinese-language article dated 17 February 2025 alleges that the compound is no longer active, though this claim is not corroborated elsewhere within the provided material.

Reporting includes screenshots and descriptions alleging extreme violence against workers inside Jinxin Park. One account describes an individual having their legs broken after attempting to escape the compound. These allegations strongly suggest coercive confinement and physical abuse. While the screenshots are not independently verified within the provided information, the severity of the claims and their consistency with known patterns of abuse in similar compounds indicate a high risk of human trafficking, forced labour, or related exploitation at the site. The site demonstrates potential signs of higher fortification, including a compound that appears walled off, a river on one side, and minimal exits. 

[Figure: Google Earth, ©2025 CNES/Airbus]

attributes.NAME__MY___comma_separated_: အိုခေပက်ခ်/ တင်းရှန်းပက်ခ်
attributes.Public_Description:
OK Park (OK园区), also referenced as Dingsheng Park, is located in Myawaddy, Kayin State, at approximately [16.709582,  98.493307] was first observed in 2018. The site is also referred to as Dingsheng Park (တင်းရှန်းပက်ခ် / 鼎盛园区), with OK Park now considered an outdated term and Dingsheng Park the more commonly used current name. According to Myanmar Witness’s verification methodology, the likelihood that OK Park is a scam compound is assessed as almost certain.

Multiple Chinese-language media articles and social media videos link the park to scam-related activity. Alleged operators or associated figures named in these sources and not included here but involved with entities referred to as Sunshine Group, Tengfei Group, and Huatian Group.

Several linked articles and social media posts contain first-person or second-hand accounts describing severe abuse connected to companies operating within the park, particularly at “Pier 20”, which is located close to the park. One set of linked messages states: “I’ve been suffering for a long time at one of the companies in Pier 20 in Myawaddy, eastern Myanmar (formerly the OK Park, now renamed the Dingsheng Park).” Additional articles report allegations of beatings, sexual assault, and other forms of mistreatment. A TikTok video further alleges that murders have occurred at the site. While these claims are not independently verified within the provided material, they are consistent across multiple platforms and sources.

Sources describe the scam compound as difficult to leave, with workers allegedly unable to exit freely; media articles and user comments on social media, including a Google Maps ‘pin’ that names the facilities ‘scamming centre’, reinforce claims that individuals working within the park are subject to coercive conditions. The site’s presence in eastern Myanmar, near the Thai border, is explicitly referenced in survivor-linked messages and media reporting included in the provided material. The facilities in the compound echo potential living quarters or office spaces seen in similar scam compounds.

[Figure: Google Earth, ©2025 CNES/Airbus]

attributes.NAME__MY___comma_separated_: ဒိုင်းမွန်းပက်ခ်
attributes.Public_Description:
Diamond Park (鑽石園區), also referred to as ဒိုင်းမွန်းပက်ခ်, is located in Myawaddy, Kayin State at approximately [16.715620, 98.499404]. The site was first observed in 2016 and is associated with the nearby or former “Star Complex.” Myanmar Witness determines that the likelihood of Diamond Park being a scam compound is high.

Business and Human Rights Resource Centre references Diamond Park in connection with human trafficking and labour abuses within telecom fraud operations in the Golden Triangle region. In this report, Diamond Park appears as part of a network of locations linked to Chinese-operated online scam and fraud activities. However, the provided material does not directly attribute specific operators or companies to the park. While no individual survivor testimonies are quoted in the provided information, the inclusion of Diamond Park in this context suggests reported concerns regarding the treatment of workers and the presence of coercive or abusive labour conditions consistent with scam compounds elsewhere in Kayin State.

Diamond Park is linked to the Star Complex casino, which was formerly a popular regional tourist attraction located within the older complex. According to a Chinese-language article dated 17 February 2025, the casino has since closed, with residents reporting that it now operates exclusively online. This reported shift away from physical tourism-oriented activity aligns with patterns seen at other sites in Myawaddy that have transitioned toward online scam or gambling operations.

Using only the provided information, Diamond Park has been linked to locations and reporting associated with telecom fraud, trafficking, and labour abuse. While the evidence does not rise to the same level of certainty as some other compounds in the area, the repeated inclusion of Diamond Park in human rights reporting and its association with the now-closed Star Complex casino support an assessment that it is likely to have functioned as, or been connected to, scam-related activities in Myawaddy, Kayin State.

[Figure: Google Earth, ©2025 CNES/Airbus]
